item.title: Loyalty Beyond Points: Why Your Rewards Program Isn't Working
item.description:
Every retailer I talk to has the same complaint: “We launched a points program, but customers aren’t engaged.”

The program works mechanically. Points accumulate. Emails go out. Redemptions happen. The dashboard shows activity. But the numbers that actually matter — repeat visit rate, basket size, net promoter score — barely moved.

Here’s the uncomfortable truth: a points program isn’t a loyalty program. It’s a discount program with extra steps.

What Loyalty Actually Is

Loyalty is a behavioral state where customers prefer you — not because you’re cheaper, not because they’ve accumulated sunk costs in your program, but because the experience of doing business with you is consistently better than the alternative.

item.title: The Data You Collect vs. the Data You Use
item.description:
In 2018, I sat in a meeting with a large retailer reviewing their customer data infrastructure. They had years of transaction history. Loyalty program data on millions of customers. Email engagement rates, web analytics, social listening. A data warehouse that cost more per year than most small businesses make in revenue.

“What do you do with all of it?” I asked.

“Mostly? Quarterly reports for the board.”

This is not unusual. In fact, it’s the norm.

item.title: What Nobody Tells You About Building B2B Software in a Niche Market
item.description:
Ten years ago, I decided to build software specifically for mid-market retail loyalty programs. Everyone I talked to had the same reaction: isn’t that too niche?

They weren’t wrong that it was niche. They were wrong that this was a problem.

The Case for Narrow

When you build for a specific market, a few things happen that don’t happen when you build for everyone.

You get very good, very fast. Customer conversations are dense with signal because the problems are specific and recurring. The person you talked to on Monday has the same underlying issue as the person on Friday — they just manifest differently. Patterns emerge quickly, and you stop reinventing the wheel with each new customer.

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m Hitesh — founder of OptCulture, where we build tools that help retailers understand and engage their customers better.

I’ve spent most of my career thinking about a deceptively simple question: what makes a customer want to come back? Not because of a discount or a push notification, but because the experience itself was worth returning to.

That question sits at the intersection of technology, behavioral psychology, and product design — which is where I tend to spend most of my time.
